drafts.title: Sediment has two independent write lanes, not a single loop
drafts.compiledTruth: The sediment write path at agent_end hook is NOT a single loop. It has two independent write lanes: Lane A/G (synchronous fence-to-write, triggered by MEMORY:/MEMORY-ABOUT-ME: fence blocks parsed from conversation, no LLM involved, directly calls writer) and Lane C (background fire-and-forget auto-write, triggered when no explicit fence blocks exist, runs LLM extractor then curator then writer, is NOT awaited by agent_end). All six new capability hooks must account for both lanes: active correction may be triggered via Lane A explicit fence or Lane C implicit extraction; outcome self-report must attach to the main-session LLM response stream; archive reactivation detection runs in Lane C.

drafts.title: Lane C auto-write is fully fire-and-forget, agent_end does not await
drafts.compiledTruth: Lane C background auto-write is explicitly NOT awaited by agent_end. The autoWriteInFlight check prevents duplicate concurrent writes. After kicking off tryAutoWriteLane(), agent_end returns immediately without waiting for LLM extraction, curation, or writer commit. This matches INV-INVISIBILITY: the user is not blocked by sediment processing. The scheduleDrainIfBacklog recursive drain loop handles backlog draining after background work completes, a natural mount point for aggregator scheduling.

drafts.title: Each Lane C draft has independent try/catch isolation
drafts.compiledTruth: Every draft in Lane C is wrapped in its own try/catch. One candidate crash does not kill other candidates in the same agent_end batch. This isolation pattern should be preserved as new capability hooks (active correction pipeline, outcome collector, staging-loader) are added; each must independently handle failure without cascading to sibling operations.

drafts.title: Seven zones are hardcoded; ZONE_META is doc-only; staging lives in project-local observations
drafts.compiledTruth: The seven-zone layout is hardcoded in brain-layout.ts (identity/skills/habits/workflows/projects/knowledge/vault). ZONE_META is documentation-only with no runtime representation. The always-vs-listed tier concept has no code model; that semantic belongs to ADR 0028 rules subsystem. The real staging location is projects/<id>/observations/staging/ (Lane G project-local, explicitly excluded from memory_search corpus). The v1-envisioned sidecar staging does not exist in code. New classifier provisional staging must choose between reusing existing project-local staging or creating a new sidecar (recommended).

drafts.title: Sanitizer is 14 deterministic regex classes with zero LLM upgrade hook
drafts.compiledTruth: extensions/sediment/sanitizer.ts implements 14 deterministic regex classes covering PEM, API keys, JWT, AWS, connection URLs, bearer tokens, Slack, Google, Stripe, generic secret assignments, and Unicode-bypass full-line redaction. It exports only sanitizeForMemory(). There is NO LLM upgrade hook: no interface to expose per-redaction original-text offset and pattern name, which would be required for the hybrid regex+LLM advisory upgrade path. Known false-redaction risk: user discussions about key patterns or PEM/JWT examples may have entire segments redacted, destroying semantic signal for classifiers. A separate abrain/redact.ts serves git-sync/prompt_user/vault/audit with non-overlapping responsibilities.

drafts.title: Audit has rich fields but lacks 6-step structured reasoning trace
drafts.compiledTruth: writer.ts audit already captures rich fields: candidates, results, curator.decision.rationale (natural language), stage_ms, background_async. What is missing is the 6-step structured trace: quote, alternative, disconfirmer, downgrade, commit, self-critique. appendAudit outputs to three points (writer main entry, about-me/world, git-sync); whether to unify these is an R2+ decision.

drafts.title: Sediment gitCommit bypasses git-sync singleFlight, risking index.lock contention
drafts.compiledTruth: git-sync.ts is transport-only (push/fetch-FF/sync with git 3-way merge, no LLM, no-origin skip, fail-no-throw). However, sediment writer's own gitCommit() does NOT go through git-sync singleFlight dedup, and can race with auto-merge for index.lock. This is a known gap that serves as evidence for ADR 0027 C2'. Not fixed in this ADR; must be acknowledged as present.

drafts.title: autoLlmWriteEnabled must retain three-state rollback: true, false, staging-only
drafts.compiledTruth: autoLlmWriteEnabled is a product rollout/rollback switch, not a source of architectural correctness. Three states must be preserved: true (full auto-write), false (no auto-write at all), and staging-only (classifier and staging run normally but no durable writes are committed). Anti-pattern MEMORY:* fences and /about-me cannot be deprecated until natural-conversation auto-write is stable, diagnosable, and rollback-capable. The default should move toward true only after dogfood validation: at least 3 users x 4 weeks, false-positive under 15%, no misbehavior complaints, staging monthly growth under 50 with over 30% resolve rate. Single-user projects may accept risk earlier with user authorization and rollback switches in place.
drafts.sourceHeading: 1.4 现有反模式 + auto-write 退路

drafts.title: ADR 0003 main-session read-only sandbox already has several breaches
drafts.compiledTruth: The main-session read-only sandbox boundary is already breached in three places: vault_release (high-value data explicit authorization, user approves each release), prompt_user (task-relevant specific decisions per ADR 0022 contract), and /secret set slash command (user-authorized vault write path). These are all write-class tools accessible to the main-session LLM. The real invariant ADR 0003 protects is 'no direct brain entry write', not 'no write-class tools at all'. This means adding brain.supersede or brain.note tools for active correction is not unprecedented; vault_release and prompt_user are already precedents for controlled write paths from the main session.

drafts.title: Design constraints are layered into A' results, B' tensions, and C' pure choices
drafts.compiledTruth: Design constraints are NOT a binary security-vs-architecture split. They are layered into three tiers: A' layer (result constraints that are unbreakable: raw secrets never pass sanitizer to LLM; vault never auto-releases without explicit authorization; staging provisional hypotheses cannot auto-promote to durable without multi-view verification). B' layer (existing mechanisms with inherent tension against ADR 0024 vision, requiring explicit R2+ evaluation of retain/redesign/exception options: ADR 0003 read-only, ADR 0018 regex sanitizer, /about-me fence anti-pattern). C' layer (pure architecture choices freely redesignable for ADR 0024 fit: seven-zone layout, memory_search corpus, curator 7-op, transport-only sync, classifier merge strategy, write loop structure). The A' layer constrains all capability designs; B' layer demands explicit decisions before proceeding.

drafts.title: Staging provisional hypotheses cannot auto-promote to durable without multi-view verification
drafts.compiledTruth: A hard A' layer constraint: staging-stored provisional hypotheses MUST NOT auto-promote to durable entries without passing through multi-view (cross-provider dual-review) verification. If multi-view is unavailable or disabled, provisionals can only stay in staging or age-out. There is ONE explicit exception: §4.1.4 conf<8 durable corrections bypass multi-view and go through curator direct-write, an explicitly accepted blast radius, not an implicit violation. The staging-to-durable gate applies strictly to the staging path only; the curator direct-write path is not constrained by this rule.

drafts.title: Confidence under 8 corrections bypass multi-view - accepted blast radius requiring prototype validation
drafts.compiledTruth: Durable corrections with confidence under 8 are routed directly through curator without multi-view verification. This is the largest blast radius in the correction pipeline: most corrections fall in the conf<8 range, yet precisely this range has zero independent verification. A classifier could misclassify casual chat as a conf=6 durable correction, bypassing all protection layers and writing directly. P1 prototype phase must specifically measure misclassification rate in the conf<8 range. If prototype shows high false-positive rates, consider raising the direct-write threshold to conf=7 or adding an intermediate staging-only path. This is an explicitly accepted risk documented as conf<8 blind-spot.
directionImpact: narrows | direction.md#INV-ACTIVE-CORRECTION | required | conf<8 durable corrections bypass multi-view verification; P1 prototype must validate false-positive rate before permanent acceptance of this blast radius

drafts.title: ADR 0003 has three conflict points with ADR 0024 vision; three resolution options evaluated
drafts.compiledTruth: ADR 0003 (main-session read-only) has three fundamental conflicts with ADR 0024: (1) latency/friction violates INV-ACTIVE-CORRECTION (user saying 'forget that entry' expects zero-latency but ADR 0003 forces sediment sidecar path with multi-turn delay); (2) outcome self-report is structurally compromised (best signal-to-noise comes from the LLM that did the work, but ADR 0003 forces a footnote+sidecar compromise, plan C); (3) defense overlap (ADR 0003 protects against prompt injection, but ADR 0024 AI-Native + multi-view + A' layer sanitizer protect against the same thing, making one layer potentially redundant). Three resolution options exist: option 1 (keep ADR 0003, accept latency, outcome via plan C), option 2 (partial relaxation: main-session LLM gets brain.* tools for active correction/outcome, gated by multi-view+sanitizer), option 3 (full redesign: no main-session vs sediment dichotomy). Default start is option 1; R2+ multi-LLM audit re-evaluates.
directionImpact: conflicts | direction.md#INV-MAIN-SESSION-READ-ONLY | proposed | Relax ADR 0003 sandbox: allow main-session LLM to call brain.* write tools for active correction and outcome self-report; gate writes through multi-view verification and sanitizer instead of rigid read-only partition
conflicts | requirements.md#REQ-005 | proposed | Same proposal: replace sediment-sidecar exclusivity with multi-view-gated direct writes from main session for correction and outcome paths

drafts.title: Regex sanitizer false-redacts semantic discussions; hybrid LLM advisory upgrade needed
drafts.compiledTruth: The 14-class regex sanitizer false-redacts when users discuss key patterns in semantic contexts (e.g. 'my API key follows sk-xxxx pattern' becomes a redaction placeholder), destroying active correction signals. Three upgrade options: option 1 (keep regex, accept false-redaction loss), option 2 (LLM-only, risk of missing raw secrets), option 3 (recommended: regex baseline preserves A' layer guarantee + LLM advisory layer re-judges regex-captured spans for semantic discussion; if so, restore original text for classifier while keeping redaction in audit/git). Current sanitizer lacks the hook points required for option 3; needs new interface exposing per-redaction original-text offset and pattern name. Upgrade timing depends on dogfood measurement of false-redaction frequency.

drafts.title: /about-me and MEMORY fence cannot be deleted until auto-write is stable and default-on
drafts.compiledTruth: The /about-me slash command and MEMORY:/MEMORY-ABOUT-ME: fence (Lane A/G) are explicit anti-patterns that violate INV-INVISIBILITY and INV-AUTONOMY by requiring user-initiated meta-work. However, they CANNOT be deleted immediately because autoLlmWriteEnabled defaults to false, making fences the only explicit memory entry point for users. The transition path: (1) ADR 0024 capabilities must be running with auto-write default-on and stable before deprecation; (2) during transition, /about-me is downgraded to advanced user diagnostic entry and suppressed from /help quickstart; (3) P6 phase performs the actual deprecation and reverse-patches ADR 0024 §4.2 anti-pattern table with a timeline.

drafts.title: Active correction must be built first - all other five capabilities depend on its output
drafts.compiledTruth: Active correction identification is the prerequisite capability that gates the quality of all five other capabilities defined in ADR 0024 §5. Outcome self-report needs to know what user feedback occurred in-task. Aggregator needs to identify correction signals per session before comparing trends across sessions. Multi-view verification triggers on active-correction-related archives. Archive reactivation detection needs to distinguish user re-adopting old preferences in natural conversation. Building active correction first is an architectural ordering constraint: the other five cannot provide reliable input without it.

drafts.title: Classifier runs on ALL lanes; every new LLM call must pass sanitizer first
drafts.compiledTruth: The active correction classifier runs on ALL agent_end invocations, no pre-filtering. It also runs on Lane A/G (explicit MEMORY: fence writes) because fence content may contain active correction semantics (e.g. 'forget the old yarn preference' inside a MEMORY: block). When Lane A/G hits the classifier, results are attached as additional metadata without blocking synchronous write. CRITICAL HARD CONSTRAINT: ALL new LLM calls, regardless of which lane they are attached to, MUST pass through sanitizeForMemory() before reaching the LLM; this is a §3.1 A' layer result constraint, not a suggestion. The classifier receives a sanitized packed_window identical to the existing llm-extractor.ts path.

drafts.title: Correction classifier prompt must enforce reasoning normalization, four readings, falsification, conservative defaults, eight bias self-checks
drafts.compiledTruth: The correction classifier prompt has five mandatory constraints that cannot be broken when modifying prompts: (1) Reasoning normalization preamble: fixed trace structure of quote-claim-alternative-uncertainty-resolving-evidence, enabling cross-model comparison for multi-view. (2) Four mandatory readings: strongest argument for each of {durable, task-local, debug, NOT-A-CORRECTION}; NOT-A-CORRECTION is mandatory and if stronger, returns null (returning null is successful operation, not laziness). (3) Falsification and downgrade: find the single best disconfirming observation first; if shallow search and no disconfirmation found, always downgrade one level. (4) Conservative defaults: uncertain durable vs task-local defaults to task-local; uncertain correction vs normal instruction defaults to signal_found=false. (5) Eight bias self-checks before output: post-hoc rationalization, sycophancy, anchoring on existing entries, over-extraction, recency, provisional-as-fact, directive-vs-correction confusion, and Chinese-English code-switch translation artifact.

drafts.title: Corrections route to three paths: durable with multi-view gate, durable conf-under-8 direct, task-local session-only
drafts.compiledTruth: Correction signals are routed based on typing and confidence into three paths: (1) Durable with confidence at least 8 goes through multi-view verification, then updates or creates entries via curator. (2) Durable with confidence under 8 goes directly to curator write without multi-view, the accepted conf<8 blind-spot. (3) Task-local corrections do not enter permanent sediment storage but are loaded into the same-session subsequent agent_end curator context as a session-local working set, cleared at session end. Debug corrections are audit.jsonl only. Complex semantics (scoped durable, identity declaration, negation) are carried by scope_description and correction_intent natural-language fields, not forced into the three types. Upgrade from task-local to durable candidate is handled by the aggregator via prompt-guided trend analysis, not a mechanical N=2 threshold. Default bias: lean task-local to avoid polluting durable zone.

drafts.title: Unattributed corrections create provisional staging entries with provenance-warning banner protecting against anchoring
drafts.compiledTruth: When active correction is detected but no corresponding entry exists (target_entry_slug is null), a provisional staging entry is created with: status=provisional, kind=provisional-correction, attribution_pending=true, originating_device for cross-device identification, and a critical _provenance_warning banner stating 'This is a classifier guess; subsequent classifiers must not treat as ground truth, must not cite as user preference, must not guide task behavior; the only legal uses are resolve or let it age.' The entry also carries hypothesis (natural language), source_utterance list, suggested_resolution_paths, and an age_signal (days_since_creation computed at read time, not a TTL). Staging lives in a sidecar directory with its own staging-loader that selects up to K=5-10 entries by semantic relevance via a lightweight LLM call at each agent_end. Staging entries are excluded from memory_search corpus. At 30 days unresolved, staging entries go through the same archive-reactivation-reviewer prompt as archive entries.

drafts.title: Staging resolve triggers on batch scan every N rounds, not passive classifier-only check
drafts.compiledTruth: Passive classifier-only check of staging_context is insufficient; most staging entries will never enter the K=5-10 window and would only be resolved at the 30-day age-out, causing a batch scan of 100+ entries with massive token cost. Three resolve trigger options: (1) Batch scan every N rounds (default N=20): staging gets resolved in 1-3 days, cost is controllable. (2) Lazy: classifier resolves only when encountering relevant context, lowest cost but unrelated staging entries never resolve. (3) Full scan every round: lowest latency but unacceptable cost. Default is option 1 with option 2 as fallback. Promote_candidate does not go directly to durable; it must go through advisory/reviewer to prevent mechanically upgrading hypotheses with insufficient attribution. Staging bloat is monitored: when directory exceeds 50 files or monthly growth exceeds 30, an advisory flag is written to audit.

drafts.title: Outcome self-report has three approaches gated by ADR 0003 resolution; default is plan C footnote+sidecar
drafts.compiledTruth: Outcome self-report (knowing whether a memory entry was used) has three approaches: Plan A (original session LLM self-reports and directly writes outcome ledger, requires ADR 0003 relaxation option 2/3). Plan B (independent sidecar LLM reads transcript and entry list after agent_end, compatible with ADR 0003 option 1). Plan C (original session LLM attaches memory-footnote fenced block after each response with decisive/confirmatory/retrieved-unused labels plus counterfactual, sediment aggregates at agent_end into a sidecar outcome-ledger JSONL file). Plan C was chosen as a structural compromise forced by ADR 0003. If ADR 0003 is relaxed (option 2/3), Plan A re-enters candidacy. The outcome-ledger is an independent sidecar, never written into entry frontmatter. Key bias caution: outcome LLMs are RLHF-trained and 'yes I used it' is the helpful answer; prompt must force counterfactual reasoning.

drafts.title: Lost memory-footnotes are never backfilled - prefer signal loss over guessed outcomes
drafts.compiledTruth: When memory-footnote blocks are lost (truncated response, session crash, LLM forgot to write), three cases are handled: (1) Entry not loaded produced no footnote: normal, conservatively treated as not used. (2) Entry was loaded but footnote is malformed (incomplete fence, JSON parse failure): audit logs outcome_footnote_parse_error, no outcome-ledger write, no guessing. (3) Session crash loses entire batch of footnotes: NEVER backfill via sidecar LLM reading transcript, because that would be Plan B's retrospective guessing (the worst compromise of ADR 0003). Lost is lost; next session will naturally produce new footnotes. The principle is prefer-loss-over-fabrication: prefer missing a few outcomes over fabricating a single false one.

drafts.title: Outcome-ledger is an independent sidecar JSONL file, never mixed into entry frontmatter
drafts.compiledTruth: Outcome data lives in an independent sidecar file outcome-ledger.jsonl with schema: timestamp, session_id, entry_slug, used (decisive/confirmatory/retrieved-unused), counterfactual (natural language), source (memory-footnote/method-a-self-report/method-b-sidecar-llm), prompt_version. It is NEVER written into entry frontmatter; this keeps the hot path clean and avoids polluting entry bodies with usage statistics. The outcome-ledger is consumed by: classifier confidence assessment (entries with correction outcomes are less trusted), aggregator cross-session trend comparison, classifier prompt evolution diagnostics, and optional multi-view sampling of outcome self-report quality.

drafts.title: Aggregator runs on drain loop with debounce; only emits advisory, never blocks writes
drafts.compiledTruth: The aggregator (cross-session trend observer) is a scheduled task mounted on the existing scheduleDrainIfBacklog recursive drain loop. It runs when background drain completes and the system is idle, checking last_aggregator_run to decide whether to trigger. Three window sizes run as independent prompts: daily, weekly, monthly. Scheduling MUST include debounce to avoid triggering cross-session analysis on every conversation turn. The aggregator is a prompt-native skeptical historian: it reads outcome data, classifier health metrics, multi-view watchdog signals, and structured context, then outputs advisory observations. It does NOT directly write staging entries, does NOT block curator operations, and does NOT interrupt writes. If aggregator fails or is killed mid-execution, it writes a degradation record and waits for the next natural retry.

drafts.title: Classifier health meta-check uses both static thresholds and 7-day trend detection
drafts.compiledTruth: After each aggregator run, an appended prompt section examines the last 50 classifier audit traces for three quality dimensions: quote rate (proportion containing verbatim quotes), alternative mention rate, and concrete self-critique rate (not generic boilerplate). Any dimension below 40% writes an advisory flag to audit.jsonl. Static thresholds alone are insufficient: a quote rate slowly declining from 95% to 85% would never trigger under-40% but the decline is meaningful. A trend detection is added: if any dimension's 7-day moving average drops at least 10 percentage points, also write an advisory flag. This is computed by the aggregator LLM itself from the 50 traces (comparing means of first 25 vs last 25), not via mechanical threshold logic. Staging bloat is also monitored: over 50 staging files or over 30/month growth rate triggers an advisory flag.

drafts.title: Multi-view verification triggers on five high-stakes write operations
drafts.compiledTruth: Multi-view verification (cross-provider dual LLM review) is triggered for five operation types: (1) confidence at least 8 creates, (2) promotion to always-tier, (3) archiving high-confidence entries, (4) cross-zone migration (e.g. preferences to maxims), and (5) durable updates triggered by user active correction. The review follows a Blind Pass 1 (reviewer sees only the claim, not proposer's reasoning) then Reveal Pass 2 (reviewer sees proposer's full reasoning and the Pass 1 result) protocol. Two independent API calls are a HARD requirement; a single call pretending to first-blind-then-reveal is not trustworthy. The reasoning normalization preamble is shared with §4.1 classifier for cross-model comparability.

drafts.title: Devil's advocate virtual reviewer layer mitigates RLHF correlation without extra API calls
drafts.compiledTruth: ADR 0024 §5.4 acknowledges that cross-model review still has RLHF training correlation; two reviewers from different model families may still err in the same direction due to shared RLHF training patterns. To mitigate this without adding API calls, Reveal Pass 2 appends a devil's-advocate self-check: the reviewer adopts the persona of a third-party from a different model family with different RLHF, formulates the strongest objection to the consensus, and judges whether the objection is a real risk (downgrade to DEFER) or a strawman (keep consensus but record in audit). This is a pure prompt-engineered layer: three virtual review rounds within two API calls. It partially mitigates but does not eliminate the RLHF correlation problem.

drafts.title: Multi-view provider routing is settings-driven, not hardcoded; starts with static fallback
drafts.compiledTruth: Multi-view cross-provider strategy is built from zero (current extractor and curator are both single-provider DeepSeek). New settings MultiViewSettings contain: proposerProviders and reviewerProviders (business model IDs in settings, never hardcoded in source), fallbackProviders for rate-limit/error handling, and costBudgetPerOpUsd as a soft budget (exceeded leads to DEFER). The minimum viable strategy starts with a static fallback list; full strategy evolves to dynamic selection with rate-limit handling and cost budgeting. Provider failure or rate-limiting triggers graceful fallback through the fallback chain. This is estimated as large+ effort, touching settings, curator, extractor, index, and audit across five files.

drafts.title: Multi-view degradation never silently falls back to proposer direct-write; user instructions deterministically committed
drafts.compiledTruth: Multi-view replay and degradation must follow strict constraints: (1) Transient reviewer failure (unavailable, failed call, unparseable, deferred) defaults to multiview-pending staging awaiting replay lane retry, NEVER silently falls back to proposer direct-write, which would break the A' layer dual-review requirement for high-value operations. (2) User-expressed Tier-1 instructions are the exception: they must be deterministically committed to proposer decision, immune to replay limits, stale cutoffs, or terminal paths that would silently swallow explicit user directives. (3) confirm_pass1_not_synthesizable can retain op=skip because Pass-1 schema lacks replayable rich payload; if Pass-1 schema expands later, re-evaluate. (4) Replay terminal path should soft-archive uncountable candidates preserving traceability; only reviewer-initiated skip can delete staging lines. (5) Replay writes must route through the same op dispatcher as the original turn; no candidate should lose its write action due to staging replay detour. (6) signal abort does not consume retry budget; abort cleans current-round new staging while preserving original candidate attempts. (7) Candidates recalculated in replay that no longer trigger multi-view should be dropped/audited, never falling back to proposer direct-write.

drafts.title: PromptVersion field must ship with P0 active correction - missing it causes soft schema migration when v2 reads v1 traces
drafts.compiledTruth: The PromptVersionSubstrate in settings must ship concurrently with P0 active correction. Each prompt (activeCorrectionClassifier, multiViewPass1/2, outcomeSelfReport, aggregator, archiveReactivationReviewer) gets a version string. Every audit.jsonl entry carries prompt_version plus its _semantic_note and a structured reasoning_trace. Without these fields, within weeks prompt v2 will encounter soft schema migration when reading old traces; the audit reader cannot distinguish version provenance. The _semantic_note enables cross-version compatibility: a new prompt reading old traces is told 'this was produced by an older prompt version, extract quote and uncertainty only, do not apply current labels.' Prompt evolution is human-in-loop: authors discover systematic blind spots, manually edit prompts, and verify against fixtures for reference (not as a release-blocking gate). Explicitly NOT done: no monthly auto prompt diff jobs, no LLM self-modifying its own prompt (closed-loop self-modification risk), no prompt accuracy threshold gate.

drafts.title: Archive reactivation requires live-use bridge detection, not mere topic mention
drafts.compiledTruth: Archive reactivation detection distinguishes between 'user mentioned the topic' and 'user is actively re-applying the preference.' The default bias is to keep archived; only a live-use bridge (current task is applying the preference and behavior matches the entry content) triggers reactivation. The decision must cite both the archived entry and the current utterance side by side. This uses the archive-reactivation-reviewer prompt, which is shared between archive reactivation detection and staging age-out resolution: one prompt serves two capability points. The N-day soft-delete window (suggested 30 days, dogfood-validated) keeps files on disk at status=archived; after N days, the final reviewer prompt decides reactivate or git rm. Archived entries are excluded from memory_search corpus during the entire soft-delete window.

drafts.title: Git rm happens only after N-day window plus final reviewer prompt judgment
drafts.compiledTruth: The archive lifecycle has three stages: (1) Soft-delete period (N days, suggested 30): status=archived, file retained on disk, excluded from memory_search corpus. (2) At N days: archive-reactivation-reviewer prompt makes final judgment, reactivate or git rm. (3) After git rm: file removed from working tree but recoverable from git history. The git rm must wait for writer-side CAS/owner-aware release to guarantee active entries are not accidentally deleted. Existing archiveProjectEntry currently does soft archive with no archive_at field and no reactivation reviewer; migration adds the archive_at ISO timestamp field, then runs a daily/weekly cron of the reviewer prompt over all archived entries within the window.

drafts.title: Archive uses absolute archive_at timestamp; cross-device sync extends window, never resets
drafts.compiledTruth: Archive cross-device synchronization uses an absolute archive_at ISO timestamp field (not local relative days). When device A archives an entry and syncs to device B, device B extends the window by archive_at + 30 days; it does NOT reset the countdown. This prevents indefinite soft-delete from repeated cross-device sync cycles. ADR 0020 transport-only sync must be reverse-patched to add archive_at field semantics. Similarly, staging provisional entries carry originating_device for cross-device identification: when device B's classifier sees a staging entry from a different device and current context is unrelated, the prompt guides it to default to 'wait for next session on originating device' rather than forcibly resolving on the wrong device.

drafts.title: Existing status=archived entries migrate by adding archive_at field and reviewer cron
drafts.compiledTruth: The current archiveProjectEntry directly calls updateProjectEntry(slug, {status:archived}): no archive_at field, no reactivation reviewer. Migration requires four steps: (1) Add archive_at ISO timestamp to archiveProjectEntry. (2) Add daily/weekly cron running archive-reactivation-reviewer prompt over all entries where status=archived AND now minus archive_at under 30d. (3) Run reactivation detection prompt to decide reactivate or continue archive. (4) For entries where now minus archive_at is at least 30d, run final reviewer prompt to decide reactivate or git rm. Staging age-out reuses the exact same archive-reactivation-reviewer prompt; the same prompt serves both staging provisional resolution and archive reactivation detection, unified through the same soft-delete then N-day window then hard-delete lifecycle.

drafts.title: Three-tier smoke tests are advisory signal only - never release-blocking gates
drafts.compiledTruth: Following ADR 0024 §3 AI-Native principle, three smoke test tiers are explicitly NOT release-blocking gates. They only leave signals for the author without blocking writes. Tier 1 (automatic assertions): structural validation, classifier output matches CorrectionSignal schema, staging fields complete, soft-archived files exist within N days, multi-view triggers produce exactly two independent API calls, every audit entry has prompt_version. Failure equals bug, must fix, infra-mechanical validation. Tier 2 (LLM-as-judge advisory): independent LLM scores reasoning traces against §4.1.3's eight bias cautions, outputs advisory flags only, no release gate. Tier 3 (information comparison dossier): side-by-side comparison of old vs new prompt outputs on real utterances when prompts change, for author review. Tier 2 and 3 explicitly do NOT block releases; they inform human judgment.
